1: שֵׁם טוֹב (šēm ṭôb, a good name) opens the verse with the word šēm — name as social reputation, the legacy of one's character in community memory. The Hebrew verb בָּחַר (bāḥar, to choose, to prefer) carries the sense of deliberate selection after evaluation; one must actively cultivate reputation rather than assume wealth will suffice. חֵן (ḥēn, favor, graciousness) — the same root that gives us the name Hanan — denotes both inner quality and the goodwill it generates in others. The parallelism equates name with favor: both are intangible social goods that outlast coined wealth.
2: עָשִׁיר וָרָשׁ נִפְגָּשׁוּ (rich and poor meet together) — פָּגַשׁ (pāgaš) is often used of unexpected or fateful meetings; here it frames the encounter of economic opposites not as accident but as creaturely fact. עֹשֵׂה כֻּלָּם יהוה (the LORD is maker of all of them) — the divine name anchors the proverb's ethics: the creator-creature relation is identical for rich and poor, making class-based contempt a form of insult toward the Maker (cf. Proverbs 17:5). The phrase resists both servile deference to wealth and envious resentment of it.
3: עָרוּם (ʿārûm, prudent, shrewd) — the same root as the serpent's cunning in Genesis 3:1, but here rehabilitated as the positive capacity for foresight. The prudent person רָאָה רָעָה (sees danger/evil) and נִסְתָּר (hides, takes cover) — the verb sātar appears in texts of divine shelter (Psalms 27:5). The naive (פְּתָאִים, pəṯāʾîm) pass on and suffer — עָנְשׁוּ from ʿānaš, to be fined, punished, literally to bear the consequence. The proverb teaches anticipatory prudence as a form of wisdom.
4: עֵקֶב עֲנָוָה יִרְאַת יהוה — עֵקֶב (ʿēqeb, the heel, consequence, result) links humility and fear of the LORD as a compound condition yielding threefold reward: עֹשֶׁר וְכָבוֹד וְחַיִּים (riches, honor, and life). עֲנָוָה (ʿanāwâ, humility, meekness) is the inward posture; יִרְאַת יהוה (fear of the LORD) is its vertical dimension. Together they constitute the disposition that opens the person to receive what God gives freely — the triad of goods is a gift to the humble, not a market transaction.
5: צִנִּים פַּחִים (thorns and snares) — a paired image of traveler hazards; both hide in the path and catch the unwary. בְּדֶרֶךְ עִקֵּשׁ (in the path of the perverse) — עִקֵּשׁ (ʿiqqēš, twisted, perverted) modifies "path" (derek) as a route that curves away from straight walking. שֹׁמֵר נַפְשׁוֹ יִרְחַק מֵהֶם (whoever guards his nephesh/life will stay far from them) — here nephesh means the whole person in mortal terms (cf. Proverbs 16:17); guarding one's life means avoiding the moral-physical terrain of the perverse.
6: חֲנֹךְ לַנַּעַר עַל-פִּי דַרְכּוֹ (train/dedicate a child according to his way) — חָנַךְ (ḥānak, to dedicate, initiate) is used of dedication of buildings (Deuteronomy 20:5) and here of childhood formation. עַל-פִּי דַרְכּוֹ (according to his way) is debated: does it mean the right way for him (his particular disposition), or simply the right path? Both senses yield the same pedagogical principle: formation must engage the actual child, not an abstraction. The promise — גַּם כִּי-יַזְקִין לֹא-יָסוּר מִמֶּנָּה — invokes lifelong formation: even when old he will not depart from it.
7: עָשִׁיר בְּרָשִׁים יִמְשׁוֹל (the rich rules over the poor) — מָשַׁל (māšal, to rule, have dominion) is the same verb used of God's intended human dominion (Genesis 1:28); here it describes the economic power differential that inverts created dignity. עֶבֶד לֹוֶה לְאִישׁ מַלְוֶה (the borrower is slave to the lender) — עֶבֶד (ʿebed, slave/servant) states the social consequence baldly. The proverb is descriptive, not prescriptive; it maps the real power dynamics of debt culture as a warning, not an endorsement.
8: זֹרֵעַ עַוְלָה יִקְצָר-אָוֶן (whoever sows injustice will reap trouble/evil) — עַוְלָה (ʿawlâ, injustice, wrongdoing) and אָוֶן (ʾāwen, trouble, wickedness, futility) form a sowing-reaping moral sequence. The metaphor is agricultural and eschatological: the harvest matches the seed. וְשֵׁבֶט עֶבְרָתוֹ יִכְלֶה (the rod/weapon of his rage will end/be broken) — שֵׁבֶט (šēbeṭ, rod, scepter, tribe) here means the instrument of violent authority; עֶבְרָה (ʿebrâ, fury, overflowing wrath) names the driving emotion. The instrument of injustice fails its wielder.
9: טוֹב-עַיִן הוּא יְבֹרָךְ (a good-eyed person — he will be blessed) — טוֹב עַיִן (good eye) is the Hebrew idiom for generosity, the opposite of the stingy רַע עַיִן (evil eye; cf. Proverbs 23:6, Proverbs 28:22). The eye as indicator of character runs throughout the ancient Near East; generosity begins in vision — in seeing the poor as a neighbor worthy of one's bread. כִּי-נָתַן מִלַּחְמוֹ לַדָּל (for he gives of his bread to the poor) — the act is voluntary sharing from one's own sustenance, not surplus-disposal.
10: גָּרֵשׁ לֵץ (drive out the mocker) — לֵץ (lēṣ, scoffer, mocker) is the figure who mocks wisdom's claim on him; removing him from the community eliminates the contagion of cynicism. The verse lists three social goods that depart with him: מָדוֹן (strife, contention), רִיב (quarreling, dispute), and קָלוֹן (dishonor, shame). The proverb identifies the scoffer as the social node that generates dysfunction — his presence is architecturally corrosive.
11: אֹהֵב טָהֳרַת-לֵב (whoever loves purity of heart) — טָהֳרַת (ṭohŏrat, purity, cleanness) is a cultic term applied here to the inner life (lēb, heart); moral purity extends from the sanctuary vocabulary to personal character. חֵן שְׂפָתָיו (grace/favor of his lips) — חֵן (ḥēn) here describes speech quality; the gracious speaker whose words carry aesthetic-moral weight. רֵעֵהוּ מֶלֶךְ (the king is his friend/companion) — the one who combines inner purity with gracious speech finds royal companionship; virtue is the true pathway to honor.
12: עֵינֵי יהוה נָצְרוּ דָעַת (the eyes of the LORD watch over/preserve knowledge) — נָצַר (nāṣar, to keep, guard, watch over) is used of God guarding his people (Psalms 31:24) and here of his attentive preservation of knowledge itself. The divine surveillance is protective toward wisdom and destructive toward its opposite: וַיְסַלֵּף דִּבְרֵי בֹגֵד (he overturns the words of the treacherous) — סָלַף (sālap̄, to twist, distort, overthrow) here means God actively subverts the treacherous person's words, making them ineffective.
13: אָמַר עָצֵל אֲרִי בַחוּץ (the sluggard says, "A lion is outside!") — the speech-act form (אָמַר, he says) highlights the absurdity: the sluggard's excuse is grotesque in its implausibility. אֲרִי בַחוּץ (a lion outside) — lions were real enough dangers, but invoking one to avoid leaving the house is comic self-deception. בְּתוֹךְ רְחֹבוֹת אֵרָצֵחַ (in the middle of the streets I will be killed) — the escalation from lion outside to murder in the streets shows the sluggard's imagination enlarging obstacles to match his will to avoid effort.
14: שׁוּחָה עֲמֻקָּה פִּי זָרוֹת (a deep pit is the mouth of foreign/adulterous women) — שׁוּחָה (šûḥâ, pit, cistern) echoes the "stranger" woman passages of chapters 1–9 where she lures the simple to Sheol. זָרוֹת (zārôt, strange/foreign women, adulterous women) carries both ethnic and moral connotation — those outside covenant loyalty. זְעוּם יהוה יִפָּל-שָׁם (one under the LORD's wrath will fall there) — the one who falls into this pit is not merely unwise but זָעַם (zāʿam, under divine displeasure); the seduction is the outworking of judgment.
15: אִוֶּלֶת קְשׁוּרָה בְלֶב-נָעַר (foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child) — קָשַׁר (qāšar, to bind, tie) implies deep attachment; ʾiwwelet (foolishness) is not superficial but structurally embedded in the young person's lēb. The word for child — נָעַר (naʿar) — covers a range from infant to young adult, the period of formation. שֵׁבֶט מוּסָר יַרְחִיקֶנָּה מִמֶּנּוּ (the rod of discipline will drive it far from him) — מוּסָר (mûsār) is the disciplinary instruction that shapes character; the rod is not mere punishment but the physical enactment of formative correction.
16: עֹשֵׁק דָּל לְהַרְבּוֹת לוֹ (oppressing the poor to increase for himself) — the infinitive לְהַרְבּוֹת marks the oppressor's motive: personal enrichment through exploitation. נֹתֵן לְעָשִׁיר אַךְ-לְמַחְסוֹר (giving to the rich — only to poverty) — both strategies are self-defeating. Giving gifts to the wealthy (to curry favor) and exploiting the poor (to build capital) both lead to מַחְסוֹר (maḥsôr, lack, poverty). The proverb inverts economic logic: the path of exploitation ends in the very deficit it sought to avoid.
17: הַט אָזְנְךָ וּשְׁמַע דִּבְרֵי חֲכָמִים (incline your ear and hear the words of the wise) — Proverbs 22:17 marks the opening of the "Words of the Wise" collection (Proverbs 22:17–Proverbs 24:22), an anthology with documented verbal parallels to the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope (c. 1200 BCE). The address form shifts from third-person proverb to direct second-person instruction. הַט (haṭ, incline, bow) — the posture of attentive reception; the ear must lean toward wisdom. וְלִבְּךָ תָּשִׁית לְדַעְתִּי (and set your heart on my knowledge) — both ear and lēb (heart) are engaged: auditory reception leads to internalized knowledge.
18: כִּי-נָעִים כִּי-תִשְׁמְרֵם בְּבִטְנֶךָ (for it is good/pleasant that you keep them in your belly/inmost self) — בֶּטֶן (beṭen, belly, womb, inward parts) is used metonymically for the deepest interior; wisdom is not a surface coating but lodged in the vital center of the person. יִכֹּנוּ יַחְדָּו עַל-שְׂפָתֶיךָ (that they be established together on your lips) — kôn (to be established, fixed, ready) implies that when wisdom is internalized, speech becomes reliable, the inner disposition expressing through the outer word.
19: לִהְיוֹת בַּיהוה בִּטְחֶךָ (that your trust may be in the LORD) — בָּטַח (bāṭaḥ, to trust, have confidence) is the relational posture that Proverbs consistently cultivates; wisdom instruction ultimately aims not at self-reliance but at anchored trust in YHWH. The teacher addresses the student directly: הוֹדַעְתִּיךָ הַיּוֹם אַף-אָתָּה (I have taught you today — even you) — the emphatic ʾattā (you) makes the instruction pointedly personal. Teaching is address, not lecture to an abstraction.
20: הֲלֹא כָתַבְתִּי לְךָ שָׁלִישִׁים — שָׁלִישִׁים is the crux of this verse; the word can mean "thirty" (pointing to the Amenemope parallel's thirty chapters), "excellent/superior things," or "officers/captains." Most scholars follow the LXX and the Amenemope connection reading "thirty sayings." The question form (הֲלֹא, "have I not?") implies the student already knows this written collection; the teacher appeals to a documented body of instruction. בְּמוֹעֵצֹת וָדָעַת (of counsel and knowledge) — the collection's content is characterized by wisdom's two governing gifts.
21: לְהוֹדִיעֲךָ קֹשְׁטְ אִמְרֵי אֱמֶת (to make you know the reliability of words of truth) — קֹשֶׁט (qōšeṭ, truth, reliability) is a rare synonym for ʾĕmet; both carry the root meaning of stability and trustworthiness rather than abstract propositional truth. אִמְרֵי אֱמֶת (words of truth) — the teaching aims to establish the student as a reliable transmitter of stable words. לְהָשִׁיב אֲמָרִים אֱמֶת (to give back/return true words) — the purpose clause shows the social function: the student is formed to speak reliably to those who sent him, as an accurate messenger.
22: אַל-תִּגְזָל-דָּל כִּי דַל-הוּא (do not rob the poor because he is poor) — the kî (because) clause makes the prohibition's rationale the very poverty of the victim; exploitation of weakness is the specific wrong named. וְאַל-תְּדַכֵּא עָנִי בַשָּׁעַר (and do not crush the afflicted at the gate) — שַׁעַר (šaʿar, gate) is the civic court of ancient Israel (Ruth 4:1–2; Amos 5:12); דָּכָא (dākāʾ, to crush, shatter) is a violent word for legal and economic oppression within the very institution meant to protect the poor.
23: כִּי-יהוה יָרִיב רִיבָם (for the LORD will plead their case) — רִיב (rîb, legal dispute, lawsuit) as both noun and verb: YHWH becomes the legal advocate (rib) for those whose rib (case) has been stolen in court. וְקָבַע אֶת-קֹבְעֵיהֶם נֶפֶשׁ (and will plunder the soul/life of those who plundered them) — קָבַע (qābaʿ, to rob, defraud) appears in Malachi 3:8 of robbing God; here it turns back on the oppressor. נֶפֶשׁ here = life; the divine plaintiff pursues the case to lethal consequence for the oppressor.
24: אַל-תִּתְרַע אֶת-בַּעַל אָף (do not be friends with one who is a master of anger) — בַּעַל אָף (master/lord of anger) uses baʿal as a term of possession: the angry man is owned by his anger. וְאֶת-אִישׁ חֵמוֹת לֹא תָבוֹא (with a man of hot passions do not associate/enter) — חֵמוֹת (ḥēmôt, burning wrath, hot passions) pairs with ʾap̄ (anger, nostrils); both images are thermological — uncontrolled anger as dangerous heat. The warning is social epidemiology: hot anger is contagious.
25: פֶּן-תֵּאֱלַף אֹרְחֹתָיו (lest you learn his paths/ways) — אָלַף (ʾālap̄, to learn, become familiar with) is the negative counterpart of wisdom's instruction; proximity to the angry man is an apprenticeship in anger. וְלָקַחְתָּ מוֹקֵשׁ לְנַפְשֶׁךָ (and you take/get a snare for your soul) — מוֹקֵשׁ (môqēš, snare, trap) is the trap set by the wise for game (cf. Ecclesiastes 9:12); here the student sets it for himself through bad association. nephesh here = the self, the whole inner person who becomes ensnared.
26: אַל-תְּהִי בְתֹקְעֵי-כָף (do not be among those who clap/strike the palm) — תָּקַע (tāqaʿ, to thrust, strike, blow a trumpet) in this idiom refers to the handshake pledge — a binding gesture in ancient Near Eastern commercial law. בַּעֹרְבִים מַשָּׁאוֹת (among those who pledge/give surety for loans) — עָרַב (ʿārab, to become surety, guarantee) and מַשָּׁאוֹת (maśśāʾôt, loans, burdens) together describe financial co-signing; the warning against surety runs throughout Proverbs (Proverbs 6:1–5; Proverbs 11:15; Proverbs 17:18).
27: אִם-אֵין לְךָ לְשַׁלֵּם (if you have nothing to pay) — the conditional sets up the economic logic: the guarantor with no assets is the most exposed position. לָמָּה יִקַּח מִשְׁכָּבְךָ מִתַּחְתֶּיךָ (why should he take your bed from under you?) — מִשְׁכָּב (miškāb, bed, place of lying) is the last possession of the destitute; the rhetorical question (lāmmāh, why?) makes the outcome seem self-inflicted. The surety arrangement robs the guarantor of sleep — metaphorically and literally.
28: אַל-תַּסֵּג גְּבוּל עוֹלָם (do not move the ancient boundary) — נָסַג (nāsag, to move back, remove) with גְּבוּל (gəbûl, border, boundary) is the act of land theft through marker-shifting (cf. Deuteronomy 19:14; Proverbs 27:17). עוֹלָם (ʿôlām, ancient, of old, the distant past) — not "eternal" here but "from time immemorial"; ancestral arrangements carry quasi-sacred authority because they encode community memory and God-given inheritance. אֲשֶׁר עָשׂוּ אֲבוֹתֶיךָ (which your ancestors set up) — the chain of generations legitimates the marker.
29: חָזִיתָ אִישׁ מָהִיר בִּמְלַאכְתּוֹ (you have seen a man skilled/swift in his work) — מָהִיר (māhîr, quick, skilled, diligent) combines speed and competence; the one who masters his craft moves with ease in it. The verse closes the chapter with a social-ascent promise: לִפְנֵי-מְלָכִים יִתְיַצָּב (before kings he will stand/present himself) — יָצַב (yāṣab, to stand, take position) before a king implies access to royal presence (cf. Genesis 41:46 of Joseph). בַּל-יִתְיַצַּב לִפְנֵי-חֲשֻׁכִּים (he will not stand before obscure people) — ḥašukkîm means the dark, the obscure, those of no social standing; excellence opens royal doors.
